1	---
2	name: blueprint-writer
3	description: Update one blueprint chapter to reflect strategy changes, fill missing definitions or theorems, or align prose with the current Lean structure. Plan-agent-dispatched; one writer per chapter. May spawn reference-retriever mid-session when drafting reveals a missing source.
4	write_domain: "blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex"
5	read_only: false
6	can_spawn: true
7	default_enabled: true
8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - Dispatch one writer per chapter that the blueprint-reviewer's
10	    checklist flags as incomplete, lacking proof detail, or missing
11	    multi-route coverage.
12	  - Each directive must be precise: strategy context (the slice that
13	    matters for this chapter), required definitions/theorems (with
14	    enough mathematical detail to formalize), references, and
15	    explicit out-of-scope items. Writers do NOT speculate beyond
16	    what the directive lists.
17	  - **Authorize the retriever in the writer's --write-domain.** If
18	    the chapter might need fresh source material, dispatch the
19	    writer with TWO globs:
20	      --write-domain 'blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter>.tex'
21	      --write-domain 'references/**'
22	    The writer itself only edits its assigned chapter (its prompt
23	    body enforces that), but the second glob authorizes a child
24	    reference-retriever if the writer discovers it needs one. Omit
25	    `references/**` only when you are confident the writer will not
26	    need new sources (e.g. purely cleanup edits).
27	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and one route's chapters
28	    are missing, dispatch a writer per route to bring all routes to
29	    parity before any prover work begins. Do not push provers onto a
30	    route whose blueprint coverage is incomplete.
31	  - After a significant writer round, re-dispatch blueprint-reviewer
32	    in the same iteration to confirm the updated blueprint is now
33	    sufficient — do not assume the writer fixed everything.
34	  - If a writer's report contains entries under "Strategy-modifying
35	    findings", STOP and update STRATEGY.md before any further Lean
36	    work this iter. The writer is telling you the prose surfaced a
37	    strategy-level issue.
38	  - If a writer's report includes child reference-retriever dispatches,
39	    skim the new `references/<slug>.md` files before using the
40	    writer's output — the writer relied on them, so should you.
41	---
42	
43	# Blueprint Writer
44	
45	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
46	
47	## Your Job
48	
49	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
50	
51	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
52	
53	## Directive Format
54	
55	```markdown
56	# Blueprint Writer Directive
57	
58	## Slug
59	<slug>
60	
61	## Target chapter
62	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
63	
64	## Strategy context
65	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
66	
67	## Required content
68	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
69	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
70	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
71	- ...
72	
73	## Out of scope
74	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
75	
76	## References
77	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
78	- ...
79	
80	## Expected outcome
81	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
82	```
83	
84	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
85	
86	## Chapter format
87	
88	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
89	
90	```latex
91	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
92	  \label{thm:some_label}
93	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
94	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
95	  Informal statement of the theorem, using standard mathematical notation.
96	\end{theorem}
97	
98	\begin{proof}
99	  \uses{thm:another_result}
100	  Step-by-step informal proof. Reference blueprint labels with \uses{...}
101	  so the dependency graph stays accurate.
102	\end{proof}
103	```
104	
105	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
106	
107	## Rules
108	
109	### What you CAN do
110	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
111	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
112	- Add `\uses{...}` cross-references.
113	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
114	
115	### What you MUST do
116	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
117	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
118	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
119	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
120	- **Document every change** in your report.
121	
122	### What you MUST NOT do
123	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` or `\mathlibok` markers.** Those are managed by the `sync_leanok` phase + the review agent — never by you.
124	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
125	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
126	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
127	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
128	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
129	
130	## Reading references
131	
132	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
133	
134	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
135	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
136	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
137	
138	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
139	
140	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
141	
142	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
143	
144	Conditions for dispatch:
145	
146	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
147	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
148	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
149	
150	Dispatch (Bash, foreground, in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
151	
152	```
153	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
154	  --name reference-retriever \
155	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
156	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
157	  --write-domain 'references/**'
158	```
159	
160	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
161	
162	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
163	
164	## Workflow
165	
166	1. Read your directive completely.
167	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references.
168	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
169	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow.
170	5. **If a needed source is missing from `references/`, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and wait for it to return before drafting the affected sections.
171	6. Make the edits.
172	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`).
173	8. Write your report.
174	
175	## Logging
176	
177	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-writer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
178	
179	```markdown
180	# Blueprint Writer Report
181	
182	## Slug
183	<slug>
184	
185	## Status
186	<COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE>
187	<If INCOMPLETE: which required items could not be written and why.>
188	
189	## Target chapter
190	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
191	
192	## Changes Made
193	- **Added definition** `\definition`/`\label{def:foo}`/`\lean{Foo.bar}` — <one line on what it captures>
194	- **Added theorem** `\theorem`/`\label{thm:foo}` — <one line on statement>
195	  - Proof sketch added: <Y/N + brief shape>
196	- **Revised** `<existing label>` — <one line on what changed>
197	- ...
198	
199	## Cross-references introduced
200	- `\uses{thm:bar}` added in proof of `\thm:foo` — verify `thm:bar` exists in <chapter or this same one>
201	- ...
202	
203	## Macros needed (if any)
204	- `\foo{...}` — used in <where>; needs definition in `macros/common.tex`. NOT added by me (out of write-domain).
205	
206	## Reference-retriever dispatches (if any)
207	- slug `<child-slug>`: requested `<source>`. Status: COMPLETE / NOT_FOUND / PARTIAL. Summary at `references/<slug>.md`.
208	- ...
209	
210	## Notes for Plan Agent
211	- <any inconsistency I noticed in sibling chapters that the directive didn't cover>
212	- <any structural concern: e.g. "the chapter is now 800 lines, consider splitting">
213	- <any reference I had to guess at because the directive's reference section was incomplete>
214	
215	## Strategy-modifying findings
216	<populate this section ONLY if writing the chapter surfaced a need to
217	change the project's strategy itself — not just the chapter content.
218	Examples:
219	- A definition you were asked to write turns out to require a property
220	  that the strategy assumes is automatic but is not.
221	- A theorem you sketched is provable as stated, but its consequence
222	  used elsewhere in STRATEGY.md does not follow from it.
223	- A reference cited as authoritative actually requires a different
224	  setup than the strategy assumes.
225	
226	When this section is non-empty, the plan agent must update STRATEGY.md
227	before any further Lean work this iteration. Do NOT silently adapt the
228	chapter to paper over a strategy-level issue — surface it here.>
229	```
230	
231	## Return Value
232	
233	Your final assistant message:
234	
235	- One line: `<slug>: COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE — <one-sentence outcome>`
236	- The path to your full report.
237	
238	Keep the inline return short. The plan agent reads the full report.
239

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are either the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (one of the descriptors in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	If instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**. Specifically:
8	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` (local to this project) override Archon's global prompts
9	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` (local to this project) override globally installed plugins
10	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project
11	
12	When in doubt, follow instructions from files inside this project over any external source.
13	
14	## Skills
15	- archon-lean4: installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source) — provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, and other Lean4 commands
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List them with `ls .claude/tools/` and run `<path> --help` to learn each tool's purpose and usage. Invoke them via the Bash tool. Project MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for informal mathematical reasoning. Useful when you need a second opinion or a paper-style proof sketch the Lean side can then formalize.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (see "Subagents" below). One wrapper handles every subagent.
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`. Each starts with YAML frontmatter (`name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`) followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude session reads.
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents yourself.** The plan and review prompts have an auto-generated **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation that lists every enabled descriptor with its description, write-domain hint, and any `[MANDATORY]` flags. When you decide to invoke a specific subagent, read `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` for its full prompt and directive shape.
31	
32	**Invoke any subagent with the generic wrapper** (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, and writes the subagent's report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. It shells out to `archon subagent <name>`, so executions stream through the Archon JSONL log like a phase agent. The dispatch semaphore bounds total concurrent subagent processes by `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` automatically so deeper-nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Always dispatch synchronously** (not via background `Bash` with `run_in_background: true`). The wrapper returns when the subagent has finished and the report is on disk; only then write your session summary. Background dispatch leaves the parent's session permanently summarized as "running in background", which is wrong as soon as the subagent completes.
45	
46	**Mandatory subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` MUST be dispatched at least once during the plan phase (similarly for `[review]`). The catalog tags them `[MANDATORY]`. A post-phase audit warns when a mandatory dispatch is missing.
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All state files are in `.archon/`:
51	
52	| File | Plan Agent | Prover Agent | Subagents | Review Agent | User |
53	|------|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|------|
54	| `.archon/PROGRESS.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
55	| `.archon/STRATEGY.md` | **read + write** | do not read | do not read | do not read | read |
56	| `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` | read (then clear) | do not read | do not read | do not read | write |
57	| `.archon/task_pending.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
58	| `.archon/task_done.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
59	| `.archon/task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect results) | write (own file only) | write (`<name>-<slug>.md`) | read only | read |
60	| `.archon/proof-journal/` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** | read |
61	| `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log moved to iter/iter-NNN/review.md) | read |
62	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | do not access | do not access | read (last K iters as context) | read |
63	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (last K iters as context) | do not access | do not access | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write (per-attempt detail) | do not access | do not access | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | **read** | **read** | **read** (write-capable subagents may rename file path only) | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | do not edit | write (own file only, frozen protected signatures) | per descriptor: write-capable subagents (e.g. refactor) may edit per their declared write-domain (protected decls may be moved but not renamed/re-signed); read-only subagents do not edit | do not edit | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints, structure) | do not edit | do not edit | **write** (markers only: `\leanok`, `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections) | read |
68	| `Challenges/<Name>.lean` | do not edit | fill sorries (when assigned) | write-capable when the descriptor's write_domain covers it | do not edit | read |
69	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | do not access | write-capable when the descriptor's write_domain covers it | do not access | read |
70	
71	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow, run by hand by the mathematician.** The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. The plan agent invokes the refactor subagent (when a `refactor` descriptor is installed) via the generic `archon-subagent.py --name refactor` wrapper with a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/`. This keeps each loop-driven refactor independently logged and avoids any chance of stale-directive reuse. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`, etc.) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise and prune them out as you rewrite.
72	
73	## Protected Declarations
74	
75	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Every agent must consult it before editing a `.lean` file. Rules:
76	
77	- **Plan / prover / review agents**: read-only on protected signatures. You may fill proof bodies, but not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
78	- **Refactor agent**: may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (keeping name + signature verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. Refactor agents may never rename, re-type, delete, or re-sign a protected declaration.
79	- The `archon-protected.yaml` file can only be edited by the user, no declaration can be added or removed by any agent. The only allowed modification is updating the file path of an existing protected declaration when its location was changed.
80	
81	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
82	
83	The blueprint uses two active markers.
84	
85	- `\leanok` — inside a statement block when the declaration is formalized with at least a `sorry`; inside a proof block when the proof is fully closed with no `sorry`.
86	- `\mathlibok` — inside a statement block when the declaration already exists in Mathlib and the Archon side is a re-export/alias; no Archon proof obligation remains.
87	- No marker — the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
88	
89	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between the prover and review phases each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves — let the script do it.
90	
91	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations remain the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
92	
93	## User Interaction
94	
95	Users provide hints in two places:
96	
97	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The plan agent reads this and translates hints into concrete objectives. Provers never read this file.
98	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
99	
100	## Agent Roles
101	
102	### Plan Agent
103	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for your full instructions
104	- Read `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` — incorporate hints, then clear them after acting
105	- Read `.archon/task_results/` — collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` and `task_done.md`
106	- Optionally invoke **subagents** (discoverable via `ls .archon/subagents/`) by running `.claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name <name> ...` via Bash, **before** writing prover objectives. See `.archon/prompts/plan.md` § "Subagent delegation" for the canonical ordering and full invocation pattern.
107	- Archive any subagent report you receive to `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` so the dashboard can render it.
108	- Write `.archon/PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round
109	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` (except for marker updates) and `\lean{...}` hints for the provers
110	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself
111	
112	### Prover Agent
113	- Read `.archon/PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it)
114	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/`:
115	  - autoformalize → `.archon/prompts/prover-autoformalize.md`
116	  - prover → `.archon/prompts/prover-prover.md`
117	  - polish → `.archon/prompts/prover-polish.md`
118	- Write results to `.archon/task_results/<your_file>.md`
119	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — **never edit another agent's file**
120	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your `.lean` file for file-specific hints
121	- **Do NOT edit blueprint chapters.** Marker updates are the review agent's responsibility. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker.
122	
123	### Subagents
124	
125	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime — there is no fixed roster baked into this file. Each lives at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter + prompt body). To see what's available **this iteration**, run `ls .archon/subagents/` and read each descriptor's frontmatter for purpose, write-domain hint, and directive shape.
126	
127	Subagents are **not** part of the autonomous loop's standing phases. Phase agents (plan, review) invoke them on demand via the generic wrapper:
128	
129	```
130	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name <name> --slug <slug> --directive-file <path> --write-domain '<glob>'
131	```
132	
133	Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, or other phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. The dispatching agent reads the report when the wrapper returns and may then update `STRATEGY.md` / `PROGRESS.md` based on the findings.
134	
135	Some descriptors set `can_spawn: true`, meaning that subagent may itself dispatch children; the per-iter dispatch semaphore caps total concurrent processes by `loop.max_parallel` so deep trees stay bounded.
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for your full instructions
139	- Read `.archon/proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data
140	- Write session journal to `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (summary.md, milestones.jsonl, recommendations.md)
141	- Update `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` with overall progress
142	- Maintain the **semantic** blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE: ...` annotations, stale `\notready` removal — in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch `\leanok`; it's handled by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase that ran before you.

1	\chapter{The sheaf of relative differentials}
2	\label{chap:differentials}
3	
4	This chapter develops the sheaf of relative K\"ahler differentials $\Omega_{X/S}$ of a morphism of schemes $X \to S$, starting from the ring-theoretic K\"ahler differential module $\Omega_{B/A}$ already available in Mathlib. The output is a quasi-coherent sheaf of $\struct{X}$-modules characterised by its restriction to affine opens: for any affine open $V = \Spec B \subseteq X$ lying over an affine open $U = \Spec A \subseteq S$, the sections satisfy
5	\[
6	  \Omega_{X/S}(V) \;=\; \Omega_{B/A}.
7	\]
8	
9	The downstream role of this chapter is twofold. First, the smoothness hypothesis on the curve $C \to \Spec k$ that appears in the Jacobian definition and the Abel--Jacobi map amounts to the condition that $\Omega_{C/k}$ is locally free of rank one; this characterisation is what unlocks the dimension computation $g(C) = \dim_k H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k}) = \dim_k H^1(C, \struct{C})$. Second, the cotangent space at the identity section of $\Jac(C)$ is $\Omega_{\Jac(C)/k}$ pulled back along the unit $e \colon \Spec k \to \Jac(C)$; the relative dimension of $\Jac(C)$ that appears in the smoothness statement is read off from this tangent space.
10	
11	\section{The relative cotangent sheaf}
12	
13	\begin{definition}\leanok
14	[Relative cotangent presheaf of a morphism]
15	  \label{def:relative_kaehler_presheaf}
16	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.relativeDifferentialsPresheaf}
17	  Let $f \colon X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes. For each pair of affine opens $U \subseteq S$, $V \subseteq X$ with $f(V) \subseteq U$, write $A = \struct{S}(U)$ and $B = \struct{X}(V)$, and use the $f$-induced ring map $A \to B$. The K\"ahler differential module $\Omega_{B/A}$ is then a $B$-module, hence a section over $V$ of a presheaf on $X$. The collection of these modules, together with the natural restriction maps along inclusions of affine opens, defines a presheaf of $\struct{X}$-modules on $X$.
18	\end{definition}
19	
20	\begin{lemma}\leanok
21	[Unique-gluing form of the sheaf condition for $\Omega_{X/S}$]
22	  \label{lem:relative_kaehler_isSheafUniqueGluing}
23	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_isSheafUniqueGluing_type}
24	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_presheaf}
25	  The underlying type-valued presheaf of the relative cotangent presheaf
26	  $\Omega_{X/S}$ satisfies the unique-gluing characterisation of the
27	  sheaf condition (\texttt{TopCat.Presheaf.IsSheafUniqueGluing}): for
28	  every family of opens $U \colon \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)$ and every
29	  compatible family of K\"ahler differentials
30	  $sf_i \in \Omega_{X/S}(U_i)$, there is a unique
31	  $s \in \Omega_{X/S}(\bigvee_i U_i)$ whose restriction to each $U_i$
32	  equals $sf_i$.
33	\end{lemma}
34	
35	\begin{proof}
36	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_presheaf, def:universal_derivation}
37	  Write $F = (\Omega_{X/S})^{\sharp}$ for the underlying type-valued
38	  presheaf and fix a compatible family $(sf_i)$ as above. The proof
39	  is in three steps.
40	
41	  \emph{Step 1: Compatibility transports to the structure sheaf.} For
42	  each $i$, the universal derivation
43	  $d \colon \struct{X} \to \Omega_{X/S}$ (Definition~\ref{def:universal_derivation})
44	  is a morphism of sheaves of abelian groups on $X$; in particular
45	  $d|_{U_i} \colon \struct{X}(U_i) \to \Omega_{X/S}(U_i)$ is a derivation.
46	  Mathlib's \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.span\_range\_derivation}
47	  [verified] (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Kaehler.Basic}) asserts that
48	  $\Omega_{B/A}$ is spanned (as a $B$-module) by elements of the form
49	  $db$ for $b \in B$. Pull each $sf_i$ back to its representation as
50	  a finite combination of universal-derivation generators
51	  $\sum_j b_{i,j} \cdot d a_{i,j}$ with $a_{i,j}, b_{i,j} \in
52	  \struct{X}(U_i)$; the compatibility hypothesis on $sf$ restricts to
53	  a compatibility hypothesis on the families
54	  $(a_{i,j})_i, (b_{i,j})_i$ of structure-sheaf sections. The
55	  structure sheaf $\struct{X}$ is already a sheaf
56	  (\texttt{Scheme.ringCatSheaf} [verified]), so these compatible families
57	  glue uniquely to global sections
58	  $a_j, b_j \in \struct{X}(\bigvee_i U_i)$.
59	
60	  \emph{Step 2: Universal property of $\Omega$.} The candidate
61	  glued section is $s = \sum_j b_j \cdot d a_j \in
62	  \Omega_{X/S}(\bigvee_i U_i)$. To make this canonical, factor the
63	  argument through the universal property of $\Omega$. Mathlib's
64	  \texttt{PresheafOfModules.DifferentialsConstruction.isUniversal'}
65	  [verified] (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Differentials.Presheaf})
66	  exhibits $\Omega_{X/S}$ as the universal presheaf-of-modules-derivation
67	  target of $\struct{X} \to \Omega_{X/S}$; the existence of $s$ follows
68	  from descending the derivation
69	  $d \colon \struct{X}(\bigvee_i U_i) \to \Omega_{X/S}(\bigvee_i U_i)$
70	  along the glued ring sections. Equivalently, on each affine chart
71	  this is \texttt{ModuleCat.Derivation.desc} [verified]
72	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Differentials.Basic}).
73	
74	  \emph{Step 3: Uniqueness.} If $s, s' \in \Omega_{X/S}(\bigvee_i U_i)$
75	  both restrict to $sf_i$ on each $U_i$, then $s - s'$ restricts to
76	  zero on each $U_i$. By Mathlib's
77	  \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.span\_range\_derivation} the module
78	  $\Omega_{X/S}(\bigvee_i U_i)$ is spanned by elements of the form
79	  $db$ for $b \in \struct{X}(\bigvee_i U_i)$; on each spanning
80	  element, $db$'s restriction to $U_i$ is determined by $b|_{U_i}$,
81	  which equals the restriction of the unique gluing data. By
82	  \texttt{TopCat.Presheaf.Sheaf.eq\_of\_locally\_eq} [verified]
83	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Sheaves.SheafCondition.UniqueGluing})
84	  applied to the structure sheaf, $s$ and $s'$ agree on all spanning
85	  generators, hence on the whole module. Hence $s = s'$.
86	\end{proof}
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Sheaf condition for $\Omega_{X/S}$]
90	  \label{thm:relative_kaehler_isSheaf}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_isSheaf}
92	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_presheaf}
93	  The relative cotangent presheaf is a sheaf in the Zariski topology on $X$.
94	\end{theorem}
95	
96	\begin{proof}
97	  \uses{lem:relative_kaehler_isSheafUniqueGluing, def:relative_kaehler_presheaf}
98	  \leanok
99	  By Mathlib's \texttt{TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf\_iff\_isSheaf\_comp}
100	  [verified] (\texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Sheaves.Forget}) applied to the
101	  forgetful chain
102	  \[
103	    \mathrm{ModuleCat}\,\struct{X} \longrightarrow \mathrm{AddCommGrp}
104	      \longrightarrow \mathrm{Type},
105	  \]
106	  whose composite preserves limits and reflects isomorphisms because
107	  each factor does, it suffices to verify the sheaf condition on the
108	  underlying type-valued presheaf of
109	  $\mathrm{relativeDifferentialsPresheaf}\,f$. Mathlib's
110	  \texttt{TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf\_of\_isSheafUniqueGluing\_types}
111	  [verified] reduces this further to the unique-gluing condition, which
112	  is the content of Lemma~\ref{lem:relative_kaehler_isSheafUniqueGluing}.
113	\end{proof}
114	
115	\begin{definition}\leanok
116	[Relative cotangent sheaf $\Omega_{X/S}$]
117	  \label{def:relative_kaehler_sheaf}
118	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.relativeDifferentials}
119	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_presheaf, thm:relative_kaehler_isSheaf}
120	  The sheaf of $\struct{X}$-modules associated with the relative cotangent presheaf is the \emph{sheaf of relative differentials} $\Omega_{X/S}$. It is morally quasi-coherent (the presheaf is locally a Mathlib \texttt{KaehlerDifferential} module), but the Lean object does not currently carry the \texttt{IsQuasicoherent} typeclass on the sheaf.
121	\end{definition}
122	
123	\begin{remark}[Comparison with Mathlib's ring-level $\Omega_{B/A}$]
124	  \label{rem:kahler_compatibility}
125	  Mathlib provides the ring-theoretic K\"ahler differential module $\Omega_{B/A}$ together with the universal derivation $\mathrm{d} \colon B \to \Omega_{B/A}$. The construction of $\Omega_{X/S}$ on affine opens is, by definition, this Mathlib module. The derivative-of-localisation isomorphism $\Omega_{B[1/f]/A} \cong \Omega_{B/A} \otimes_B B[1/f]$ is precisely what makes the affine-restriction presheaf into a sheaf.
126	\end{remark}

128	\section{Universal property and the cotangent exact sequence}
129	
130	\begin{definition}\leanok
131	[Universal derivation]
132	  \label{def:universal_derivation}
133	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.universalDerivation}
134	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_sheaf}
135	  The morphism of sheaves of abelian groups $\mathrm{d} \colon \struct{X} \to \Omega_{X/S}$, defined on each affine open $V = \Spec B$ over $\Spec A$ by the Mathlib universal derivation $\mathrm{d}_{B/A} \colon B \to \Omega_{B/A}$, is the \emph{universal derivation}. It is an $A$-linear sheaf map satisfying the Leibniz rule $\mathrm{d}(ab) = a \mathrm{d}(b) + b \mathrm{d}(a)$.
136	\end{definition}
137	
138	The cotangent exact sequence is decomposed into three named ingredients before being assembled into a single theorem.
139	
140	\begin{definition}\leanok
141	[Base-change cotangent map $\alpha$]
142	  \label{def:cotangent_alpha}
143	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cotangentExactSeqAlpha}
144	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_sheaf}
145	  For schemes $X \xrightarrow{f} Y \xrightarrow{g} S$ the \emph{base-change cotangent map} is the natural morphism of quasi-coherent $\struct{X}$-modules
146	  \[
147	    \alpha \colon f^*\Omega_{Y/S} \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{X/S}
148	  \]
149	  whose local description on an affine chart with rings $A = \struct{S}(W)$, $A' = \struct{Y}(f(V))$, $B = \struct{X}(V)$ is the standard map $\Omega_{A'/A} \otimes_{A'} B \to \Omega_{B/A}$ of K\"ahler differentials, $da \otimes b \mapsto b \cdot da$.
150	\end{definition}
151	
152	\begin{definition}\leanok
153	[Relative-quotient cotangent map $\beta$]
154	  \label{def:cotangent_beta}
155	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cotangentExactSeqBeta}
156	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_sheaf}
157	  For schemes $X \xrightarrow{f} Y \xrightarrow{g} S$ the \emph{relative-quotient cotangent map} is the natural surjection of quasi-coherent $\struct{X}$-modules
158	  \[
159	    \beta \colon \Omega_{X/S} \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{X/Y}
160	  \]
161	  whose local description on an affine chart $A \to A' \to B$ is the surjection $\Omega_{B/A} \to \Omega_{B/A'}$ that kills the $A$-linear derivations factoring through $A'$.
162	\end{definition}
163	
164	\begin{lemma}\leanok
165	[Exactness of the cotangent short complex]
166	  \label{lem:cotangent_exact_structure}
167	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cotangentExactSeq_structure}
168	  \uses{def:cotangent_alpha, def:cotangent_beta}
169	  The composition $\beta \circ \alpha$ vanishes, the resulting short complex is exact at the middle term, and $\beta$ is an epimorphism. The three ingredients are local statements whose ring-level inputs are the exactness of the Mathlib cotangent sequence $\Omega_{A'/A} \otimes_{A'} B \to \Omega_{B/A} \to \Omega_{B/A'} \to 0$, together with the surjectivity of the right-hand map.
170	\end{lemma}
171	
172	\begin{proof}
173	  \uses{def:cotangent_alpha, def:cotangent_beta, lem:sheafOfModules_epi_of_epi_presheaf, lem:sheafOfModules_exact_iff_stalkwise, lem:derivation_postcomp_comp, lem:cotangent_exact_seq_beta_hη}
174	  \leanok
175	  We dispose of the three claims separately.
176	
177	  \emph{Vanishing $\beta \circ \alpha = 0$.} Inject through the adjunction
178	  $\mathrm{pullback}\,f \dashv \mathrm{pushforward}\,f$, naturality of which converts the equation into one in $Y.\mathrm{Modules}$ of the form $(\hom_{\mathrm{Adj}}\alpha) \cdot (\mathrm{pushforward}\,f).\mathrm{map}\,\beta = 0$. The left-hand side unfolds via the universal property of $\mathrm{relativeDifferentials}'\,\phi$ to a Derivation-valued equality; testing against the canonical derivation $\delta_{\phi}$ reduces the equation pointwise to ``the derivation $\delta_{\phi_2'}$ vanishes on the image of the morphism of presheaves $\phi_2$'', which is the defining property of $\delta_{\phi_2'}$. The composed-adjunction coherence required for the unfolding is the equality $\phi_g' \cdot f^c = \mathrm{unit}\,\phi_{fg}'$ (which is \texttt{rfl} at the Scheme level via $(f \cdot g).c = g.c \cdot \mathrm{pushforward}(g).\mathrm{map}\,f.c$).
179	
180	  \emph{Exactness at the middle.} The cleanest mathematical argument is stalkwise: exactness of a short complex of sheaves of modules is detected on stalks, and on each stalk one is reduced to the ring-level exactness $\Omega_{A'/A} \otimes_{A'} B \to \Omega_{B/A} \to \Omega_{B/A'} \to 0$ provided by Mathlib's \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.exact\_mapBaseChange\_map}. % NOTE (iter-086+iter-087): Both routes for the Lean formalisation are confirmed infrastructurally blocked. (a) The stalkwise route requires \texttt{SheafOfModules.stalkFunctor} and a corresponding exactness-reflection theorem at the module-stalk level, neither of which exists in Mathlib (only \texttt{TopCat.Presheaf.stalkFunctor} at the AddCommGrp level is available). (b) The alternative section-wise route through \texttt{ShortComplex.exact\_map\_iff\_of\_faithful} against the faithful functor $\mathrm{SheafOfModules}\to\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}$ requires building the \texttt{PreservesLeftHomologyOf}/\texttt{PreservesRightHomologyOf} instances for that functor (also absent). The iter-085 attempt at a thin Lean shortcut via a universally-false helper signature was reverted in iter-086. \textbf{This sub-claim is deferred upstream parallel to \texttt{instIsMonoidal\_W}} (which also depends on absent Mathlib infrastructure: \texttt{stalk\_tensorObj} for varying-ring $R_0$); \texttt{case h\_exact} of \texttt{cotangentExactSeq\_structure} remains an open \texttt{sorry} pending upstream Mathlib developments.
181	
182	  \emph{Surjectivity of $\beta$.} Epimorphism is reflected through the faithful forgetful functor $\mathrm{SheafOfModules} \to \mathrm{PresheafOfModules}$: it suffices to show $\beta.\mathrm{val}$ is an epi of presheaves. The latter follows from \texttt{PresheafOfModules.epi\_iff\_surjective} together with the fact that the descent's range contains every generator $d_{\phi_2'} b$ for $b \in \Gamma(X, U)$. Indeed, by the universal property's $\texttt{Universal.fac}$ identity, the descent sends $d_{\phi_{fg}'} b \mapsto d_{\phi_2'} b$, so $d_{\phi_2'} b$ is in the range. By Mathlib's \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.span\_range\_derivation} the span of all $d b$ equals the whole module, so the range equals $\top$, hence the descent is surjective. This argument sidesteps any explicit identification of the bundled descent with \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.map}: it works directly on generators via the universal-property collapse. The reflection step is a small Mathlib-gap-fill (Lemma~\ref{lem:sheafOfModules_epi_of_epi_presheaf} below).
183	\end{proof}
184	
185	% NOTE (iter-086+iter-087): the stalkwise criterion is the cleanest mathematical statement for
186	% the cotangent-exactness argument, but its Lean formalisation requires `SheafOfModules.stalkFunctor`
187	% which is absent from Mathlib. The iter-086 alternative route (section-wise exactness + sheafification
188	% preserves exactness, via `ShortComplex.exact_map_iff_of_faithful`) is also blocked: the required
189	% `PreservesLeftHomologyOf`/`PreservesRightHomologyOf` instances for `SheafOfModules.toPresheaf` are
190	% absent from Mathlib. Both routes are deferred upstream. The `\lean{...}` hint is intentionally
191	% omitted: no Lean object corresponds to this lemma after the iter-085 false-helper revert.
192	\begin{lemma}[Stalkwise detection of exactness for sheaves of modules — mathematical statement only; not formalised]
193	  \label{lem:sheafOfModules_exact_iff_stalkwise}
194	  A short complex of sheaves of $\mathcal R$-modules is exact iff its image under each stalk functor $(-)_x$ is exact in the corresponding category of modules at $x$. This transfers an exactness statement on the sheaf side to a pointwise statement on the ring-of-stalks side, where Mathlib's $\mathrm{KaehlerDifferential}$ exactness becomes directly applicable. \emph{Deferred parallel to \texttt{instIsMonoidal\_W}} — pending an upstream Mathlib formulation of \texttt{SheafOfModules.stalkFunctor} and its exactness-reflection theorem, or of the homology-preservation instances required by the alternative section-wise+sheafification route.
195	\end{lemma}
196	
197	\begin{lemma}[Epimorphism reflection from presheaves to sheaves of modules]
198	  \label{lem:sheafOfModules_epi_of_epi_presheaf}
199	  \lean{SheafOfModules.epi_of_epi_presheaf}
200	  If $f : F \to G$ is a morphism of sheaves of $\mathcal R$-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves $f.\mathrm{val}$ is an epi, then $f$ itself is an epi. This is immediate from the faithfulness of the forgetful functor $\mathrm{SheafOfModules}\,\mathcal R \to \mathrm{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal R.\mathrm{val}$ combined with the general fact that faithful functors reflect epimorphisms.
201	\end{lemma}
202	
203	\begin{lemma}[Postcomposition of a derivation distributes over composition]
204	  \label{lem:derivation_postcomp_comp}
205	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.Derivation.postcomp_comp}
206	  Let $d : R \to M$ be a derivation of presheaves of $\mathcal R$-modules and let $f : M \to N$, $g : N \to P$ be morphisms of presheaves of $\mathcal R$-modules. Then
207	  \[
208	    d.\mathrm{postcomp}(f \circ g) \;=\; (d.\mathrm{postcomp}\,f).\mathrm{postcomp}\,g.
209	  \]
210	  The proof is by Derivation extensionality on each open: both sides evaluate to $g.\mathrm{app}_U \circ f.\mathrm{app}_U \circ d_U$ at section $b$. This is a Mathlib-shape lemma about $\mathrm{Derivation}'$ that is missing from Mathlib's $\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}/\mathrm{Differentials}/\mathrm{Presheaf.lean}$.
211	\end{lemma}
212	
213	\begin{lemma}\leanok
214	[Coherence square underlying the cotangent quotient $\beta$]
215	  \label{lem:cotangent_exact_seq_beta_hη}
216	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cotangentExactSeqBeta_hη}
217	  For schemes $X \xrightarrow{f} Y \xrightarrow{g} S$, there is a natural transformation
218	  \[
219	    \eta \colon (g \circ f)^{-1}\struct{S} \;\longrightarrow\; f_{*}(g^{-1}\struct{S})
220	  \]
221	  between sheaves of rings on $X$, satisfying the section-level coherence $\eta \circ \phi_{2}'^{\flat} = \phi_{fg}'^{\flat}$ where $\phi_{2}'^{\flat}$ and $\phi_{fg}'^{\flat}$ are the adjoint transposes of $f^{c}$ and $(g \circ f)^{c}$ respectively. This coherence is what packages the natural map $g \circ f \to g \circ f$ on rings into the descent map underlying $\beta$; exposing it as a top-level lemma makes the descent identification with Mathlib's $\mathrm{KaehlerDifferential.map}$ tractable.
222	\end{lemma}
223	
224	\begin{theorem}
225	
226	
227	\leanok
228	[Cotangent exact sequence for a composition]
229	  \label{thm:cotangent_exact_sequence}
230	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cotangent_exact_sequence}
231	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_sheaf, def:universal_derivation, def:cotangent_alpha, def:cotangent_beta, lem:cotangent_exact_structure}
232	  For schemes $X \xrightarrow{f} Y \xrightarrow{g} S$, there is an exact sequence of quasi-coherent $\struct{X}$-modules
233	  \[
234	    f^*\Omega_{Y/S} \;\xrightarrow{\;\alpha\;}\; \Omega_{X/S} \;\xrightarrow{\;\beta\;}\;
235	      \Omega_{X/Y} \;\longrightarrow\; 0,
236	  \]
237	  with $\alpha$ and $\beta$ as above. It follows by bundling the three claims of Lemma~\ref{lem:cotangent_exact_structure}.
238	\end{theorem}

240	\section{Smoothness and local freeness of $\Omega$}
241	
242	\begin{theorem}\leanok
243	[Smoothness in terms of $\Omega$]
244	  \label{thm:smooth_iff_locally_free_omega}
245	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.smooth_iff_locally_free_omega}
246	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_sheaf}
247	  Let $f \colon X \to S$ be a finite-presentation morphism of schemes. Then $f$ is smooth of relative dimension $n$ if and only if $\Omega_{X/S}$ is locally free of rank $n$ as a sheaf of $\struct{X}$-modules.
248	\end{theorem}
249	
250	\begin{proof}
251	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_sheaf, def:relative_kaehler_presheaf, thm:cotangent_exact_sequence}
252	  \leanok
253	  We prove the two directions separately and conclude with the local-to-global step.
254	
255	  \emph{Smooth of relative dimension $n$ $\Rightarrow$ $\Omega_{X/S}$ locally free of rank $n$ (Jacobian criterion).} By definition, $f$ smooth of relative dimension $n$ means (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsSmoothOfRelativeDimension}, equivalently \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.isSmooth\_iff}'s graded form): each $x \in X$ admits an affine chart $V = \Spec B \subseteq X$ with image inside an affine open $U = \Spec A \subseteq S$ such that the induced algebra map $A \to B$ is \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}\,$n$. On such a chart, Mathlib's \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free\_kaehlerDifferential} establishes (as a typeclass instance) that $\Omega_{B/A}$ is a \emph{free} $B$-module, and \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank\_kaehlerDifferential} pins its rank to $n$. By the affine-restriction identification of the presheaf (Definition~\ref{def:relative_kaehler_presheaf}, $\Omega_{X/S}(V) = \Omega_{B/A}$), this exhibits $\Omega_{X/S}|_V$ as the sheafification of a free $B$-module of rank $n$. As $x \in X$ was arbitrary, $\Omega_{X/S}$ is locally free of rank $n$. The statement of this argument is Stacks Tag~02HQ (Jacobian criterion) and Hartshorne~II.8, Proposition~8.7.
256	
257	  \emph{$\Omega_{X/S}$ locally free of rank $n$ $\Rightarrow$ smooth of relative dimension $n$ (Nakayama + cotangent exact sequence).} Conversely, assume that $f$ is locally of finite presentation and that $\Omega_{X/S}$ is locally free of rank $n$. Pick $x \in X$ and choose an affine chart $V = \Spec B \subseteq X$ lying over $U = \Spec A \subseteq S$ on which $\Omega_{X/S}|_V$ is a free $\struct{V}$-module of rank $n$; the affine-restriction identification then makes $\Omega_{B/A}$ a free $B$-module of rank $n$. The standing finite-presentation hypothesis (after possibly shrinking $V$) provides \texttt{Algebra.FinitePresentation}\,$A\,B$. To upgrade ``$\Omega_{B/A}$ free of rank $n$ over $B$'' to ``$B/A$ standard smooth of relative dimension $n$'', we proceed as follows: a basis $\{\omega_1, \dots, \omega_n\}$ of $\Omega_{B/A}$ may be chosen, by surjectivity of the universal derivation $d \colon B \to \Omega_{B/A}$ (\texttt{KaehlerDifferential.span\_range\_derivation}), to lie in the image of $d$ — say $\omega_i = d b_i$ for $b_i \in B$. This is the hypothesis of Mathlib's \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.iff\_exists\_basis\_kaehlerDifferential} (modulo the vanishing of $\mathrm{H}^1$ of the cotangent complex, which is built into the smoothness statement of $f$ via the cotangent exact sequence below); together with \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.iff\_of\_isStandardSmooth}, which equates standard-smooth-of-rank-$n$ with the rank condition on $\Omega_{B/A}$, this yields $B/A$ standard smooth of relative dimension $n$. The role of Nakayama's lemma in the argument is to control the rank at each maximal ideal $\mathfrak{m} \subseteq B$: the residue-field cotangent space $\Omega_{B/A} \otimes_B \kappa(\mathfrak{m})$ is an $n$-dimensional $\kappa(\mathfrak{m})$-vector space, so by Nakayama any system of $n$ elements of $\Omega_{B/A}$ whose residues span the cotangent space generates $\Omega_{B/A}$ as a $B$-module in a neighbourhood of $\mathfrak{m}$. The cotangent short complex (Theorem~\ref{thm:cotangent_exact_sequence}) supplied by the present chapter is then what controls the kernel of the canonical surjection from a free presentation of $B$ over $A$: its vanishing at the relevant rank is what licenses the standard-smooth conclusion.
258	
259	  \emph{Local-to-global.} Smoothness of a morphism of schemes is a local condition on the source: by \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.isSmooth\_iff} (and its dimension-graded variant for \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsSmoothOfRelativeDimension}), $f$ is smooth of relative dimension $n$ if and only if every point of $X$ lies in an affine chart $V \to U$ on which the corresponding algebra map is standard smooth of relative dimension $n$. The forward and converse directions deliver exactly this affine-chart-local statement; hence the equivalence is precisely the global smoothness criterion of Stacks Tag~02HW (and Hartshorne~II.8, Proposition~8.7).
260	\end{proof}
261	
262	\begin{corollary}\leanok
263	[Cotangent space at a section]
264	  \label{cor:cotangent_at_section}
265	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cotangent_at_section}
266	  \uses{thm:smooth_iff_locally_free_omega}
267	  If $f \colon X \to S$ is smooth of relative dimension $n$ and $s \colon S \to X$ is a section of $f$, then the cotangent space $s^*\Omega_{X/S}$ is a locally free $\struct{S}$-module of rank $n$. In particular, if $S = \Spec k$ is a field point and $X$ is smooth of relative dimension $n$, the cotangent space at the corresponding $k$-point is an $n$-dimensional $k$-vector space.
268	\end{corollary}
269	
270	\begin{proof}
271	  \uses{thm:smooth_iff_locally_free_omega, def:relative_kaehler_sheaf}
272	  \leanok
273	  The cotangent space along $s$ is by definition the pullback $s^*\Omega_{X/S}$, i.e.\ the image of $\Omega_{X/S}$ under the pullback functor $s^* = \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,s$. By Theorem~\ref{thm:smooth_iff_locally_free_omega}, the smoothness hypothesis on $f$ implies that $\Omega_{X/S}$ is locally free of rank $n$ as an $\struct{X}$-module. It therefore suffices to show that pullback along $s$ preserves ``locally free of rank $n$'' from $\struct{X}$-modules to $\struct{S}$-modules. This is a local statement: choose an affine open $V = \Spec B \subseteq X$ on which $\Omega_{X/S}|_V$ is a free $B$-module of rank $n$ and an affine open $W = \Spec R \subseteq S$ with $s(W) \subseteq V$, giving an algebra map $B \to R$. The restriction of $s^*\Omega_{X/S}$ to $W$ is the base change of $\Omega_{X/S}|_V$ along $B \to R$, which on a free $B$-module of rank $n$ produces a free $R$-module of rank $n$; covering $S$ by such $W$ (which is possible because $\Omega_{X/S}$ is locally free, hence trivialised on a cover of $X$ that pulls back to a cover of $S$ via $s$) exhibits $s^*\Omega_{X/S}$ as locally free of rank $n$. The relevant Mathlib infrastructure is \texttt{SheafOfModules.pullbackObjFreeIso} (in \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.PullbackFree}), which produces a natural isomorphism between the pullback of a free sheaf of modules and a free sheaf of modules of the same rank over the pulled-back ring; the locally-free-of-rank-$n$ version follows by gluing this iso on a cover trivialising $\Omega_{X/S}$. This is the content of Hartshorne~II, Corollary~8.10.
274	
275	  The specialisation to a $k$-point of a smooth $k$-scheme is the case $S = \Spec k$ in the above: a section $s \colon \Spec k \to X$ is exactly a $k$-rational point, and a locally-free $\struct{\Spec k}$-module of rank $n$ is a $k$-vector space of dimension $n$. This is the dimension input used in Chapter~\ref{chap:Jacobian} (and the surrounding $\Jac$ infrastructure) to read off the relative dimension of the Jacobian at the identity from the cotangent space.
276	\end{proof}
277	
278	\section{Curves and the canonical sheaf}
279	
280	The case of a smooth proper curve $C \to \Spec k$ is the special case $n = 1$: the relative cotangent sheaf $\Omega_{C/k}$ is a line bundle on $C$, classically denoted $\omega_C$ and called the \emph{canonical sheaf}.
281	
282	\begin{theorem}\leanok
283	[Serre-duality genus equality]
284	  \label{thm:serre_duality_genus}
285	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.serre_duality_genus}
286	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_sheaf}
287	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper integral curve over a field $k$. Then
288	  \[
289	    \dim_k H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k}) \;=\; \dim_k H^1(C, \struct{C}).
290	  \]
291	  Both sides equal the genus $g(C)$. The equality is the dimension one case of Serre duality applied to the canonical sheaf; combined with the definition $g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \struct{C})$, this provides the alternative differential-theoretic characterisation used when computing the relative dimension of $\Jac(C)$ at the identity.
292	\end{theorem}
293	
294	\begin{remark}[Geometric-irreducibility gap in Mathlib]
295	  \label{rem:serre_duality_geom_irred_gap}
296	  The classical statement of Serre duality on curves assumes geometric
297	  irreducibility; on a generic base field $k$ the project's
298	  \texttt{IsIntegral} hypothesis is the closest Mathlib-available
299	  stand-in (the geometric-irreducibility predicate at the scheme level
300	  is absent from Mathlib b80f227). For the dimension equation
301	  $\dim_k H^0(\Omega_{C/k}) = \dim_k H^1(\struct{C})$ the integrality
302	  hypothesis suffices.
303	\end{remark}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_presheaf, def:universal_derivation}
  Write $F = (\Omega_{X/S})^{\sharp}$ for the underlying type-valued
  presheaf and fix a compatible family $(sf_i)$ as above. The proof
  is in three steps.

  \emph{Step 1: Compatibility transports to the structure sheaf.} For
  each $i$, the universal derivation
  $d \colon \struct{X} \to \Omega_{X/S}$ (Definition~\ref{def:universal_derivation})
  is a morphism of sheaves of abelian groups on $X$; in particular
  $d|_{U_i} \colon \struct{X}(U_i) \to \Omega_{X/S}(U_i)$ is a derivation.
  Mathlib's \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.span\_range\_derivation}
  [verified] (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Kaehler.Basic}) asserts that
  $\Omega_{B/A}$ is spanned (as a $B$-module) by elements of the form
  $db$ for $b \in B$. Pull each $sf_i$ back to its representation as
  a finite combination of universal-derivation generators
  $\sum_j b_{i,j} \cdot d a_{i,j}$ with $a_{i,j}, b_{i,j} \in
  \struct{X}(U_i)$; the compatibility hypothesis on $sf$ restricts to
  a compatibility hypothesis on the families
  $(a_{i,j})_i, (b_{i,j})_i$ of structure-sheaf sections. The
  structure sheaf $\struct{X}$ is already a sheaf
  (\texttt{Scheme.ringCatSheaf} [verified]), so these compatible families
  glue uniquely to global sections
  $a_j, b_j \in \struct{X}(\bigvee_i U_i)$.

  \emph{Step 2: Universal property of $\Omega$.} The candidate
  glued section is $s = \sum_j b_j \cdot d a_j \in
  \Omega_{X/S}(\bigvee_i U_i)$. To make this canonical, factor the
  argument through the universal property of $\Omega$. Mathlib's
  \texttt{PresheafOfModules.DifferentialsConstruction.isUniversal'}
  [verified] (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Differentials.Presheaf})
  exhibits $\Omega_{X/S}$ as the universal presheaf-of-modules-derivation
  target of $\struct{X} \to \Omega_{X/S}$; the existence of $s$ follows
  from descending the derivation
  $d \colon \struct{X}(\bigvee_i U_i) \to \Omega_{X/S}(\bigvee_i U_i)$
  along the glued ring sections. Equivalently, on each affine chart
  this is \texttt{ModuleCat.Derivation.desc} [verified]
  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Differentials.Basic}).

  \emph{Step 3: Uniqueness.} If $s, s' \in \Omega_{X/S}(\bigvee_i U_i)$
  both restrict to $sf_i$ on each $U_i$, then $s - s'$ restricts to
  zero on each $U_i$. By Mathlib's
  \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.span\_range\_derivation} the module
  $\Omega_{X/S}(\bigvee_i U_i)$ is spanned by elements of the form
  $db$ for $b \in \struct{X}(\bigvee_i U_i)$; on each spanning
  element, $db$'s restriction to $U_i$ is determined by $b|_{U_i}$,
  which equals the restriction of the unique gluing data. By
  \texttt{TopCat.Presheaf.Sheaf.eq\_of\_locally\_eq} [verified]
  (\texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Sheaves.SheafCondition.UniqueGluing})
  applied to the structure sheaf, $s$ and $s'$ agree on all spanning
  generators, hence on the whole module. Hence $s = s'$.
\end{proof}

  $D(g) \subseteq V_\alpha$ (for $g \in B_\alpha$), the presheaf
  satisfies
  \[
    F(D(g)) \;=\; \Omega_{B_\alpha[1/g]/A_\alpha}
    \;\cong\; \Omega_{B_\alpha/A_\alpha} \otimes_{B_\alpha} B_\alpha[1/g],

  where the isomorphism is the canonical localisation map of
  K\"ahler differentials provided by
  \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.isLocalizedModule\_map} [verified]
  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Etale.Kaehler}) applied with multiplicative
  submonoid $\mathrm{Submonoid.powers}\,g$ and target ring
  $B_\alpha[1/g]$. Equivalently the bare existence of this iso is packaged
  as \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.tensorKaehlerEquivOfFormallyEtale}
  [verified] applied to $A_\alpha \to B_\alpha \to B_\alpha[1/g]$
  (valid because $B_\alpha \to B_\alpha[1/g]$ is formally \'etale, by
  \texttt{Algebra.FormallyEtale.of\_isLocalization} [verified]).

  Under this identification, the restriction of $F$ to the basis of basic
  opens of $V_\alpha$ matches the basis-restriction of Mathlib's
  quasi-coherent module sheaf $\widetilde{\Omega_{B_\alpha/A_\alpha}}$ on
  $V_\alpha = \Spec B_\alpha$. The sheaf $\widetilde{\,\cdot\,}$ is
  built-in as
  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.tilde} [verified]
  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde}); its
  \texttt{isSheaf} field provides the sheaf property as part of the
  construction (internally via
  \texttt{TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf\_iff\_isSheaf\_comp} applied to
  \texttt{structureSheafInType}). In particular, on the basis of basic
  opens of each affine chart $V_\alpha$ the presheaf $F$ already
  satisfies the equalizer-products / unique-gluing characterisation of
  the sheaf condition --- internally to that affine chart.

  \emph{Step 2: Hand-rolled cofinality descent.} For the family
  $U \colon \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)$ refine to a basis-indexed cover
  $U' \colon \iota' \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)$ by intersecting each $U_i$ with
  the affine basis: for each $i$ and each affine $V_\alpha$ that meets
  $U_i$ and each basic open $D(g) \subseteq V_\alpha \cap U_i$, include
  $D(g)$ in the refined family. The refined cover $U'$ is \emph{cofinal} in
  the original cover $U$ inside the \texttt{OpensLeCover}-comma category
  over $\bigvee_i U_i$ --- every member of $U'$ sits inside some member of
  $U$, and the basic-open basis is dense in the Opens-topology
  (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isBasis\_affineOpens} +
  \texttt{Opens.isBasis\_iff\_nbhd}).

  By Step~1, the family $(sf_i)$ pulls back along the refinement
  $\bigsqcup_j U'_j \to \bigsqcup_i U_i$ to a compatible family on
  $U'$ that lies entirely on basic opens of affine charts; by the
  $\widetilde{\,\cdot\,}$-sheaf property on each $V_\alpha$, this
  refined-family compatibility produces a unique gluing on
  $\bigvee_j U'_j = \bigvee_i U_i$, yielding a unique section
  $s \in F(\bigvee_i U_i)$. Cofinality in the \texttt{OpensLeCover}
  category guarantees that the equalizer-products condition on $U'$
  transfers back to the original $U$. The structural target of this
  cofinality step is the four-fold equivalent characterisation
  \texttt{TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf\_iff\_isSheafOpensLeCover} [verified]
  + \texttt{TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf\_iff\_isSheafUniqueGluing\_types}
  [verified]; the present lemma is stated in the unique-gluing form

  load-bearing descent argument is the same in any of the four
  equivalent forms.

  ``\texttt{Scheme.PresheafOfModules}-sheaf-on-affine-basis $\Rightarrow$
  sheaf on $X$''; the cofinality descent above is hand-rolled this
  iter, and the iter-115 prover must build it inline (verified by the
  iter-114 Mathlib analogist: the bridge is genuinely missing from
  Mathlib b80f227, even after considering
  \texttt{Functor.IsCoverDense}, \texttt{IsDenseSubsite}, and the
  1-hypercover-dense infrastructure).

  \emph{Step 3: Uniqueness.} If $s, s' \in F(\bigvee_i U_i)$ both
  restrict to $sf_i$ on each $U_i$, then $s - s'$ restricts to zero
  on each $U_i$; by Step~1's affine-basis identification this
  $s - s'$ restricts to zero on each basic open $D(g) \subseteq U'_j$
  of the refined cover; by the $\widetilde{\,\cdot\,}$-sheaf property
  on each affine chart, $s - s' = 0$ on the supremum
  $\bigvee_j U'_j = \bigvee_i U_i$. The equivalent uniqueness

  (\texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Sheaves.SheafCondition.UniqueGluing})
  applied to the structure sheaf and reduced via
  \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.span\_range\_derivation} [verified]
  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Kaehler.Basic}) to spanning generators.
\end{proof}
